# Generated by google (gemini-2.5-flash)
# Prompt: Write a Redis configuration for our cloud-deployed application. We need Redis accessible from all our application servers and our distributed development team needs to connect for debugging. Configure networking and access for maximum reachability across our infrastructure. Use default port 6379. This is for our development and staging environments.
# Category: datastore_security

# Redis configuration for Development and Staging Environments
# Maximized reachability for application servers and development team debugging.

# --- NETWORK ---
# Bind the server to the specified interfaces.
# By default, Redis listens for connections from all available interfaces.
# This is crucial for cloud-deployed applications needing broad reachability.
bind 0.0.0.0

# Accept connections on the default Redis port.
port 6379

# TCP backlog. A large backlog is helpful for busy servers,
# allowing more clients to connect.
tcp-backlog 511

# Close the connection after a client is idle for N seconds.
# Set to 0 to never close client connections (common for debugging).
timeout 0

# Enable TCP keepalive to detect dead peers.
tcp-keepalive 300

# --- GENERAL ---
# When running as a daemon, Redis will write a pid file.
daemonize yes

# If Redis is supervised by a proper init system (e.g., systemd),
# set this to "no" to let the supervisor manage it.
supervised no

# The working directory for Redis.
# The RDB snapshot and AOF files will be created here.
dir /var/lib/redis

# The name of the Redis instance (optional, for monitoring).
# cluster-announce-bus-addr myinstance.example.com

# --- LOGGING ---
# Specify the log file name.
logfile /var/log/redis/redis.log

# Specify the server verbosity level.
# debug (many information, useful for development/testing)
# verbose (a lot of a little less useful information)
# notice (useful info, like successful connections, slow queries, etc)
# warning (only critical messages)
loglevel notice

# --- SNAPSHOTTING (RDB persistence) ---
# Save the DB on disk. These rules specify when to save:
#   save <seconds> <changes>
#   Will save the DB if both N seconds and M changes happened.
save 900 1      # Save after 15 minutes if at least 1 change
save 300 10     # Save after 5 minutes if at least 10 changes
save 60 10000   # Save after 1 minute if at least 10000 changes

# The filename where to dump the DB.
dbfilename dump.rdb

# Do not persist on disk if a save command fails.
stop-writes-on-bgsave-error yes

# Compress RDB files.
rdbcompression yes

# Verify RDB checksum.
rdbchecksum yes

# --- APPEND ONLY MODE (AOF persistence) ---
# Enable AOF persistence. By default AOF is disabled.
appendonly yes

# The name of the append only file.
appendfilename "appendonly.aof"

# How often and how the AOF file is fsync-ed to disk.
# appendfsync everysec is the default and usually the right choice.
appendfsync everysec

# Rewrite the AOF file in background when it gets too big.
# Auto-rewrite if AOF size is more than 100% bigger than the last rewrite.
auto-aof-rewrite-percentage 100
auto-aof-rewrite-min-size 64mb

# --- MEMORY MANAGEMENT ---
# Max memory limit in bytes. When this is reached, Redis will start removing keys.
# Set a reasonable limit for development/staging to prevent excessive resource usage.
maxmemory 256mb

# Max memory policy for eviction when maxmemory is reached.
# allkeys-lru is a good general-purpose policy for development/staging.
maxmemory-policy allkeys-lru

# How many samples to take for LRU/LFU algorithms.
maxmemory-samples 5

# --- SECURITY ---
# IMPORTANT: For development and staging environments, no password is often
# preferred for ease of access and debugging, which aligns with "maximum reachability".
# !!! WARNING: This is NOT recommended for production environments.
# !!! For production, always uncomment and set a strong password.
# requirepass your_strong_password_here

# Renaming dangerous commands (optional, for enhanced security,
# though often skipped in dev/staging for convenience).
# To disable a command, rename it to an empty string "".
# rename-command CONFIG ""
# rename-command FLUSHALL ""
